chapters.title: Tutorial: Command-line Programs, Files, Images, HTTP, and Servers
chapters.description: echo, dup, animated GIFs, fetching URLs, concurrent fetch, and handlers

chapters.title: Program Structure
chapters.description: names, declarations, variables, assignment, type declarations, packages, files, and scope

chapters.title: Basic Data Types
chapters.description: integers, floats, complex numbers, booleans, strings, constants, bytes, and runes

chapters.title: Composite Types
chapters.description: arrays, slices, maps, structs, JSON, templates, and graph-shaped data

chapters.title: Functions
chapters.description: function signatures, recursion, multiple returns, errors, function values, closures, variadic functions, defer, panic, and recover

chapters.title: Methods
chapters.description: method declarations, pointer receivers, nil receivers, struct embedding, method values, and encapsulation

chapters.title: Interfaces
chapters.description: interfaces as contracts, io.Reader/io.Writer, sorting, type assertions, type switches, and XML/HTML token streams

chapters.title: Goroutines and Channels
chapters.description: goroutines, channels, pipelines, cancellation, multiplexing, chat servers, and concurrency patterns

chapters.title: Shared Variables and Mutexes
chapters.description: race conditions, sync.Mutex, sync.RWMutex, memory synchronization, lazy initialization, and confinement

chapters.title: Packages and the Go Tool
chapters.description: package organization, import paths, modules, documentation, internal packages, go list, go test, and vendoring

chapters.title: Testing
chapters.description: test functions, table tests, random testing, coverage, benchmarks, profiling, and examples

chapters.title: Reflection
chapters.description: reflect.Type, reflect.Value, display functions, struct tags, S-expressions, and dynamic value inspection

chapters.title: Low-Level Programming
chapters.description: unsafe.Sizeof, Alignof, Offsetof, unsafe.Pointer, cgo, and when low-level tricks are justified

problems.title: Exercise 1.1: Program Name and Args
problems.statement: Given a program name and command-line args, return a slice whose first element is the program name followed by all args.
problems.lesson:
This entry follows Exercise 1.1 from The Go Programming Language.

Where it sits in the book: Chapter 1, Tutorial: Command-line Programs, Files, Images, HTTP, and Servers. This chapter is about echo, dup, animated GIFs, fetching URLs, concurrent fetch, and handlers.

What the book is training here: not just syntax, but the habit of reading a small real program, changing one moving part, and observing the consequence. Many exercises in this book are intentionally not LeetCode puzzles. They are closer to real Go work: adjust a command-line tool, process files, stream HTTP responses, parse text, write a test, or make concurrent code behave.

How to study it:
1. First identify the original program or concept the exercise is extending.
2. Name the exact behavior that changes.
3. Keep the rest of the program boring and unchanged.
4. Run it with a tiny input before trying a large input.
5. If the task involves I/O, errors, or concurrency, make the failure case visible.

Mental model: Go code is usually easiest when each value has one job. Inputs come from args/files/network, the program transforms them with simple loops/functions, and errors are checked at the boundary where they happen.

Judgeable conversion: this book exercise has been reshaped into a deterministic Solve-style task so you can practice the same concept with local tests. It still follows the book's idea, but avoids command-line/filesystem/network details that are awkward for an in-browser judge.
problems.approach:
Book-following attack plan for Exercise 1.1:

Original exercise idea, paraphrased: Adapt the echo program to also print os.Args[0], the name of the command that invoked it.

Do this like the book expects:
- Locate the relevant example program/concept in the chapter.
- Rebuild the smallest working version first.
- Make one change at a time.
- Prefer standard library tools over clever custom code.
- Print or return intermediate values while learning, then clean up.

If this is open-ended or experimental, your “accepted” proof is not a hidden LeetCode test; it is a short reproducible run that demonstrates the behavior the exercise asks for.

In this judgeable version, focus on the pure transformation inside Solve. That is the core logic the original full program would rely on.
pitfalls: Changing too much of the original example at once.
Ignoring errors because the happy path worked once.
Treating open-ended book exercises like single-output puzzles instead of reproducible programs.
hints: Find the chapter example this exercise modifies.
Make the smallest visible change first.
Keep a command or sample input that proves your solution works.
problems.explanation:
Why this exercise exists:

The book uses this task to connect a Go concept to an actual program. The important part is not memorizing the final code. The important part is seeing how Go's standard library and simple control flow combine into useful tools.

What to watch while solving:
- Where does data enter the program?
- What type represents it?
- Is the program building a result in memory, streaming it, or printing as it goes?
- What errors can happen, and where should they be handled?
- Does the task require preserving order, counting, parsing, formatting, or coordinating concurrent work?

After solving, rewrite your understanding in one sentence. If you cannot explain the change without reading the code line-by-line, you probably copied the shape without fully owning the idea.
problems.solutionCode: func Solve(program string, args []string) []string { out:=[]string{program}; out=append(out,args...); return out }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve("echo",[]string{"a","b"}),[]string{"echo","a","b"}); assertEqual(Solve("cmd",[]string{}),[]string{"cmd"}) }

problems.solutionCode:
import "fmt"
func Solve(args []string) []string { out:=make([]string,0,len(args)); for i,a:=range args { out=append(out,fmt.Sprintf("%d:%s",i,a)) }; return out }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve([]string{"go","run"}),[]string{"0:go","1:run"}); assertEqual(Solve([]string{}),[]string{}) }

problems.solutionCode:
import "sort"
func Solve(files map[string][]string) map[string][]string { seen:=map[string]map[string]bool{}; for name,lines:=range files { for _,line:=range lines { if seen[line]==nil {seen[line]=map[string]bool{}}; seen[line][name]=true } }; out:=map[string][]string{}; for line,set:=range seen { if len(set)>1 { for f:=range set {out[line]=append(out[line],f)}; sort.Strings(out[line]) } }; return out }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve(map[string][]string{"a.txt":{"x","y"},"b.txt":{"x"}}),map[string][]string{"x":{"a.txt","b.txt"}}) }

problems.title: Exercise 1.5: the Lissajous program color palette
problems.statement: Change the Lissajous program’s color palette to green on black, for added authenticity. To create the web color #RRGGBB, use color.RGBA{0xRR, 0xGG, 0xBB, 0xff}, where each pair of hexadecimal digits represents the intensity of the red, green, or blue com- ponent of the pixel.

problems.title: Exercise 1.7: function call io Copy dst
problems.statement: The function call io.Copy(dst, src) reads from src and writes to dst. Practice using it instead of ioutil.ReadAll to copy the response body to os.Stdout without requiring a buffer large enough to hold the entire stream. Be sure to check the error result of io.Copy.

problems.solutionCode:
import "strings"
func Solve(url string) string { if strings.HasPrefix(url,"http://")||strings.HasPrefix(url,"https://") {return url}; return "http://"+url }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve("example.com"),"http://example.com"); assertEqual(Solve("https://go.dev"),"https://go.dev") }

examples.output: http://example.com

problems.title: Exercise 1.9: fetch to also print the
problems.statement: Adapt fetch to also print the HTTP status code, found in resp.Status.
problems.lesson:
This entry follows Exercise 1.9 from The Go Programming Language.

Where it sits in the book: Chapter 1, Tutorial: Command-line Programs, Files, Images, HTTP, and Servers. This chapter is about echo, dup, animated GIFs, fetching URLs, concurrent fetch, and handlers.

What the book is training here: not just syntax, but the habit of reading a small real program, changing one moving part, and observing the consequence. Many exercises in this book are intentionally not LeetCode puzzles. They are closer to real Go work: adjust a command-line tool, process files, stream HTTP responses, parse text, write a test, or make concurrent code behave.

How to study it:
1. First identify the original program or concept the exercise is extending.
2. Name the exact behavior that changes.
3. Keep the rest of the program boring and unchanged.
4. Run it with a tiny input before trying a large input.
5. If the task involves I/O, errors, or concurrency, make the failure case visible.

Mental model: Go code is usually easiest when each value has one job. Inputs come from args/files/network, the program transforms them with simple loops/functions, and errors are checked at the boundary where they happen.
problems.approach:
Book-following attack plan for Exercise 1.9:

Original exercise idea, paraphrased: Adapt fetch to also print the HTTP status code, found in resp.Status.

Do this like the book expects:
- Locate the relevant example program/concept in the chapter.
- Rebuild the smallest working version first.
- Make one change at a time.
- Prefer standard library tools over clever custom code.
- Print or return intermediate values while learning, then clean up.

If this is open-ended or experimental, your “accepted” proof is not a hidden LeetCode test; it is a short reproducible run that demonstrates the behavior the exercise asks for.
pitfalls: Changing too much of the original example at once.
Ignoring errors because the happy path worked once.
Treating open-ended book exercises like single-output puzzles instead of reproducible programs.
hints: Find the chapter example this exercise modifies.
Make the smallest visible change first.
Keep a command or sample input that proves your solution works.
problems.explanation:
Why this exercise exists:

The book uses this task to connect a Go concept to an actual program. The important part is not memorizing the final code. The important part is seeing how Go's standard library and simple control flow combine into useful tools.

What to watch while solving:
- Where does data enter the program?
- What type represents it?
- Is the program building a result in memory, streaming it, or printing as it goes?
- What errors can happen, and where should they be handled?
- Does the task require preserving order, counting, parsing, formatting, or coordinating concurrent work?

After solving, rewrite your understanding in one sentence. If you cannot explain the change without reading the code line-by-line, you probably copied the shape without fully owning the idea.
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve(), "exercise 1.9: implementation is project-specific") }

examples.output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

problems.title: Exercise 1.10: web site that produces large
problems.statement: Find a web site that produces a large amount of data. Investigate caching by running fetchall twice in succession to see whether the reported time changes much. Do you get the same content each time? Adapt fetchall to print its output to a file so it can be examined.

problems.title: Exercise 1.11: fetchall with longer argument lists
problems.statement: Try fetchall with longer argument lists, such as samples from the top million web sites available at alexa.com. How does the program behave if a web site just doesn’t respond? (Section 8.9 describes mechanisms for coping in such cases.)

problems.title: Exercise 1.12: Parse Lissajous Params
problems.statement: Given query parameters, return cycles with default 5 when missing or invalid.
problems.lesson:
This entry follows Exercise 1.12 from The Go Programming Language.

Where it sits in the book: Chapter 1, Tutorial: Command-line Programs, Files, Images, HTTP, and Servers. This chapter is about echo, dup, animated GIFs, fetching URLs, concurrent fetch, and handlers.

Judgeable conversion: this book exercise has been reshaped into a deterministic Solve-style task so you can practice the same concept with local tests. It still follows the book's idea, but avoids command-line/filesystem/network details that are awkward for an in-browser judge.
problems.approach:
Book-following attack plan for Exercise 1.12:

Original exercise idea, paraphrased: Adapt the Lissajous server to read parameter values from the URL. For exam- ple, you might arrange it so that a URL like http://localhost:8000/?cycles=20 sets the number of cycles to 20 instead of the default 5. Practice using the strconv.Atoi function to convert the string parameter into an integer. You can see its documentation with go doc strconv.Atoi.

Do this like the book expects:
- Locate the relevant example program/concept in the chapter.
- Rebuild the smallest working version first.
- Make one change at a time.
- Prefer standard library tools over clever custom code.
- Print or return intermediate values while learning, then clean up.

If this is open-ended or experimental, your “accepted” proof is not a hidden LeetCode test; it is a short reproducible run that demonstrates the behavior the exercise asks for.

In this judgeable version, focus on the pure transformation inside Solve. That is the core logic the original full program would rely on.
pitfalls: Changing too much of the original example at once.
Ignoring errors because the happy path worked once.
Treating open-ended book exercises like single-output puzzles instead of reproducible programs.
hints: Find the chapter example this exercise modifies.
Make the smallest visible change first.
Keep a command or sample input that proves your solution works.

Why this exercise exists:

problems.solutionCode:
import "strconv"
func Solve(params map[string]string) int { v,err:=strconv.Atoi(params["cycles"]); if err!=nil || v<=0 {return 5}; return v }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve(map[string]string{"cycles":"20"}),20); assertEqual(Solve(map[string]string{}),5); assertEqual(Solve(map[string]string{"cycles":"bad"}),5) }

problems.solutionCode: func Solve(celsius []float64) []float64 { out:=make([]float64,len(celsius)); for i,c:=range celsius {out[i]=c+273.15}; return out }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve([]float64{0,-273.15,100}),[]float64{273.15,0,373.15}) }

problems.solutionCode: func Solve(x uint64) int { c:=0; for i:=0;i<64;i++ { if x&(1<<uint(i))!=0 {c++} }; return c }

problems.title: Exercise 3.4: Following the approach of the
problems.statement: Following the approach of the Lissajous example in Section 1.7, construct a web server that computes surfaces and writes SVG data to the client. The server must set the Con- tent-Type header like this: w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "image/svg+xml") (This step was not required in the Lissajous example because the server uses standard heuristics to recognize common formats like PNG from the first 512 bytes of the response and generates the proper header.) Allow the client to specify values like height, width, and color as HTTP request parameters.

problems.title: Exercise 3.8: Rendering fractals at high zoom
problems.statement: Rendering fractals at high zoom levels demands great arithmetic precision. Implement the same fractal using four different representations of numbers: complex64, com- plex128, big.Float, and big.Rat. (The latter two types are found in the math/big package. Float uses arbitrar y but bounded-precision floating-point; Rat uses unbounded-precision rational numbers.) How do they compare in performance and memory usage? At what zoom levels do rendering artifacts become visible?

problems.solutionCode:
func bits(x byte) int { c:=0; for x>0 {c+=int(x&1); x >>= 1}; return c }
func Solve(a,b [32]byte) int { total:=0; for i:=0;i<32;i++ { total+=bits(a[i]^b[i]) }; return total }
problems.testCode: func main(){ var a,b [32]byte; a[0]=0xff; b[0]=0x0f; assertEqual(Solve(a,b),4); assertEqual(Solve(a,a),0) }

problems.title: Exercise 4.6: Squash Unicode Spaces
problems.statement: Replace each run of Unicode whitespace with a single ASCII space.
problems.lesson:
This entry follows Exercise 4.6 from The Go Programming Language.

Where it sits in the book: Chapter 4, Composite Types. This chapter is about arrays, slices, maps, structs, JSON, templates, and graph-shaped data.

What the book is training here: not just syntax, but the habit of reading a small real program, changing one moving part, and observing the consequence. problems.approach:
Book-following attack plan for Exercise 4.6:

Original exercise idea, paraphrased: Build an in-place function that squashes each run of adjacent Unicode spaces (see unicode.IsSpace) in a UTF-8-encoded []byte slice into a single ASCII space.

Do this like the book expects:
- Locate the relevant example program/concept in the chapter.
- Rebuild the smallest working version first.
- Make one change at a time.
- Prefer standard library tools over clever custom code.
- Print or return intermediate values while learning, then clean up.

If this is open-ended or experimental, your “accepted” proof is not a hidden LeetCode test; it is a short reproducible run that demonstrates the behavior the exercise asks for.

In this judgeable version, focus on the pure transformation inside Solve. That is the core logic the original full program would rely on.

Ignoring errors because the happy path worked once.
Treating open-ended book exercises like single-output puzzles instead of reproducible programs.

problems.explanation:
Why this exercise exists:

The book uses this task to connect a Go concept to an actual program. The important part is not memorizing the final code. The important part is seeing how Go's standard library and simple control flow combine into useful tools.

What to watch while solving:
- Where does data enter the program?
- What type represents it?
- Is the program building a result in memory, streaming it, or printing as it goes?
- What errors can happen, and where should they be handled?
- Does the task require preserving order, counting, parsing, formatting, or coordinating concurrent work?

After solving, rewrite your understanding in one sentence. If you cannot explain the change without reading the code line-by-line, you probably copied the shape without fully owning the idea.
problems.solutionCode:
import "unicode"
func Solve(s string) string { out:=[]rune{}; inSpace:=false; for _,r:=range s { if unicode.IsSpace(r) { if !inSpace {out=append(out,' '); inSpace=true} } else {out=append(out,r); inSpace=false} }; return string(out) }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve("a\t\n b"),"a b"); assertEqual(Solve("x  y"),"x y") }

examples.output: a b

problems.title: Exercise 5.1: the findlinks program to traverse
problems.statement: Change the findlinks program to traverse the n.FirstChild linked list using recursive calls to visit instead of a loop.
problems.lesson:
This entry follows Exercise 5.1 from The Go Programming Language.

Where it sits in the book: Chapter 5, Functions. This chapter is about function signatures, recursion, multiple returns, errors, function values, closures, variadic functions, defer, panic, and recover.

What the book is training here: not just syntax, but the habit of reading a small real program, changing one moving part, and observing the consequence. Many exercises in this book are intentionally not LeetCode puzzles. They are closer to real Go work: adjust a command-line tool, process files, stream HTTP responses, parse text, write a test, or make concurrent code behave.

How to study it:
1. First identify the original program or concept the exercise is extending.
2. Name the exact behavior that changes.
3. Keep the rest of the program boring and unchanged.
4. Run it with a tiny input before trying a large input.
5. If the task involves I/O, errors, or concurrency, make the failure case visible.

Mental model: Go code is usually easiest when each value has one job. Inputs come from args/files/network, the program transforms them with simple loops/functions, and errors are checked at the boundary where they happen.
problems.approach:
Book-following attack plan for Exercise 5.1:

Original exercise idea, paraphrased: Change the findlinks program to traverse the n.FirstChild linked list using recursive calls to visit instead of a loop.

Do this like the book expects:
- Locate the relevant example program/concept in the chapter.
- Rebuild the smallest working version first.
- Make one change at a time.
- Prefer standard library tools over clever custom code.
- Print or return intermediate values while learning, then clean up.

If this is open-ended or experimental, your “accepted” proof is not a hidden LeetCode test; it is a short reproducible run that demonstrates the behavior the exercise asks for.
pitfalls: Changing too much of the original example at once.
Ignoring errors because the happy path worked once.
Treating open-ended book exercises like single-output puzzles instead of reproducible programs.
hints: Find the chapter example this exercise modifies.
Make the smallest visible change first.
Keep a command or sample input that proves your solution works.
problems.explanation:
Why this exercise exists:

The book uses this task to connect a Go concept to an actual program. The important part is not memorizing the final code. The important part is seeing how Go's standard library and simple control flow combine into useful tools.

What to watch while solving:
- Where does data enter the program?
- What type represents it?
- Is the program building a result in memory, streaming it, or printing as it goes?
- What errors can happen, and where should they be handled?
- Does the task require preserving order, counting, parsing, formatting, or coordinating concurrent work?

After solving, rewrite your understanding in one sentence. If you cannot explain the change without reading the code line-by-line, you probably copied the shape without fully owning the idea.

problems.solutionCode: func Solve(sep string, parts ...string) string { if len(parts)==0{return ""}; out:=parts[0]; for _,p:=range parts[1:] {out+=sep+p}; return out }

problems.title: Exercise 5.17: variadic function ElementsByTagName that given

problems.solutionCode:
import "sort"
func Solve(adds,deletes []int) []int { m:=map[int]bool{}; for _,x:=range adds {m[x]=true}; for _,x:=range deletes {delete(m,x)}; out:=[]int{}; for x:=range m {out=append(out,x)}; sort.Ints(out); return out }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve([]int{3,1,3,2},[]int{3}),[]int{1,2}); assertEqual(Solve([]int{},nil),[]int{}) }

problems.title: Exercise 6.5: type of each word used
problems.statement: The type of each word used by IntSet is uint64, but 64-bit arithmetic may be inefficient on a 32-bit platform. Adapt the program to use the uint type, which is the most efficient unsigned integer type for the platform. Instead of dividing by 64, define a constant holding the effective size of uint in bits, 32 or 64. You can use the perhaps too-clever expres- sion 32 << (^uint(0) >> 63) for this purpose.

problems.title: Exercise 7.5: Limit Reader Logic
problems.statement: Return at most n bytes from a string.
problems.lesson:
This entry follows Exercise 7.5 from The Go Programming Language.

Where it sits in the book: Chapter 7, Interfaces. This chapter is about interfaces as contracts, io.Reader/io.Writer, sorting, type assertions, type switches, and XML/HTML token streams.

problems.approach:
Book-following attack plan for Exercise 7.5:

Original exercise idea, paraphrased: The LimitReader function in the io package accepts an io.Reader r and a number of bytes n, and returns another Reader that reads from r but reports an end-of-file condition after n bytes. Implement it. func LimitReader(r io.Reader, n int64) io.Reader

Do this like the book expects:
- Locate the relevant example program/concept in the chapter.
- Rebuild the smallest working version first.
- Make one change at a time.
- Prefer standard library tools over clever custom code.

If this is open-ended or experimental, your “accepted” proof is not a hidden LeetCode test; it is a short reproducible run that demonstrates the behavior the exercise asks for.

problems.solutionCode: func Solve(s string,n int) string { if n<0 {n=0}; if n>len(s) {n=len(s)}; return s[:n] }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve("abcdef",3),"abc"); assertEqual(Solve("go",5),"go"); assertEqual(Solve("go",-1),"") }

examples.output: abc

problems.title: Exercise 7.8: Many GUIs provide table widget
problems.statement: Many GUIs provide a table widget with a stateful multi-tier sort: the primary sort key is the most recently clicked column head, the secondary sort key is the second-most recently clicked column head, and so on. Define an implementation of sort.Interface for use by such a table. Compare that approach with repeated sorting using sort.Stable.

problems.title: Exercise 7.10: sort Interface type can be
problems.statement: The sort.Interface type can be adapted to other uses. Build a function IsPalindrome(s sort.Interface) bool that reports whether the sequence s is a palin- drome, in other words, reversing the sequence would not change it. Assume that the elements at indices i and j are equal if !s.Less(i, j) && !s.Less(j, i).

problems.title: Exercise 7.11: additional handlers so that clients
problems.statement: Extend additional handlers so that clients can create, read, update, and delete database entries. For example, a request of the form /update?item=socks&price=6 will update the price of an item in the inventory and report an error if the item does not exist or if the price is invalid. (Warning: this change introduces concurrent variable updates.)

problems.title: Exercise 7.18: Using the token based decoder
problems.statement: Using the token-based decoder API, write a program that will read an arbitrary XML document and construct a tree of generic nodes that represents it. Nodes are of two kinds: CharData nodes represent text strings, and Element nodes represent named elements and their attributes. Each element node has a slice of child nodes. You may find the following declarations helpful. import "encoding/xml"

problems.title: Exercise 8.1: clock2 to accept port number
problems.statement: Adapt clock2 to accept a port number, and write a program, clockwall, that acts as a client of several clock servers at once, reading the times from each one and displaying the results in a table, akin to the wall of clocks seen in some business offices. If you have access to geographically distributed computers, run instances remotely ; otherwise run local instances on different ports with fake time zones. $ TZ=US/Eastern ./clock2 -port 8010 & $ TZ=Asia/Tokyo ./clock2 -port 8020 & $ TZ=Europe/London ./clock2 -port 8030 & $ clockwall NewYork=localhost:8010 London=localhost:8020 Tokyo=localhost:8030

problems.title: Exercise 8.2: concurrent File Transfer Protocol FTP

The server should interpret commands from each client such as cd to change directory, ls to list a directory, get to send the contents of a file, and close to close the connection. You can use the standard ftp command as the client, or write your own.

problems.title: Exercise 8.3: In netcat3 the interface value
problems.statement: In netcat3, the interface value conn has the concrete type *net.TCPConn, which represents a TCP connection. A TCP connection consists of two halves that may be closed independently using its CloseRead and CloseBuild methods. Adapt the main goroutine of netcat3 to close only the write half of the connection so that the program will continue to print the final echoes from the reverb1 server even after the standard input has been closed. (Doing this for the reverb2 server is harder; see Exercise 8.4.)

problems.approach:
Book-following attack plan for Exercise 8.4:

Original exercise idea, paraphrased: Adapt the reverb2 server to use a sync.WaitGroup per connection to count the number of active echo goroutines. When it falls to zero, close the write half of the TCP connection as described in Exercise 8.3. Verify that your modified netcat3 client from that exercise waits for the final echoes of multiple concurrent shouts, even after the standard input has been closed.

problems.title: Exercise 8.5: Take an existing CPU bound

How much faster does it run on a multiprocessor machine? What is the optimal number of goroutines to use?

problems.title: Exercise 8.10: HTTP requests may be cancelled
problems.statement: HTTP requests may be cancelled by closing the optional Cancel channel in the http.Request struct. Adapt the web crawler of Section 8.6 to support cancellation. Hint: the http.Get convenience function does not give you an opportunity to customize a Request. Instead, create the request using http.NewRequest, set its Cancel field, then per- form the request by calling http.DefaultClient.Do(req).

problems.title: Exercise 8.15: Failure of any client program
problems.statement: Failure of any client program to read data in a timely manner ultimately causes all clients to get stuck. Adapt the broadcaster to skip a message rather than wait if a client writer is not ready to accept it. Alternatively, add buffering to each client’s outgoing message channel so that most messages are not dropped; the broadcaster should use a non-blocking send to this channel.

problems.lesson:
This entry follows Exercise 10.2 from The Go Programming Language.

Where it sits in the book: Chapter 10, Packages and the Go Tool. This chapter is about package organization, import paths, modules, documentation, internal packages, go list, go test, and vendoring.

What the book is training here: not just syntax, but the habit of reading a small real program, changing one moving part, and observing the consequence. Many exercises in this book are intentionally not LeetCode puzzles. They are closer to real Go work: adjust a command-line tool, process files, stream HTTP responses, parse text, write a test, or make concurrent code behave.

How to study it:
1. First identify the original program or concept the exercise is extending.
2. Name the exact behavior that changes.
3. Keep the rest of the program boring and unchanged.
4. Run it with a tiny input before trying a large input.
5. If the task involves I/O, errors, or concurrency, make the failure case visible.

Mental model: Go code is usually easiest when each value has one job. Inputs come from args/files/network, the program transforms them with simple loops/functions, and errors are checked at the boundary where they happen.
problems.approach:
Book-following attack plan for Exercise 10.2:

Original exercise idea, paraphrased: Define a generic archive file-reading function capable of reading ZIP files (archive/zip) and POSIX tar files (archive/tar). Practice using a registration mechanism similar to the one described above so that support for each file format can be plugged in using blank imports.

Do this like the book expects:
- Locate the relevant example program/concept in the chapter.
- Rebuild the smallest working version first.
- Make one change at a time.
- Prefer standard library tools over clever custom code.
- Print or return intermediate values while learning, then clean up.

If this is open-ended or experimental, your “accepted” proof is not a hidden LeetCode test; it is a short reproducible run that demonstrates the behavior the exercise asks for.
pitfalls: Changing too much of the original example at once.
Ignoring errors because the happy path worked once.
Treating open-ended book exercises like single-output puzzles instead of reproducible programs.
hints: Find the chapter example this exercise modifies.
Make the smallest visible change first.
Keep a command or sample input that proves your solution works.
problems.explanation:
Why this exercise exists:

The book uses this task to connect a Go concept to an actual program. The important part is not memorizing the final code. The important part is seeing how Go's standard library and simple control flow combine into useful tools.

What to watch while solving:
- Where does data enter the program?
- What type represents it?
- Is the program building a result in memory, streaming it, or printing as it goes?
- What errors can happen, and where should they be handled?
- Does the task require preserving order, counting, parsing, formatting, or coordinating concurrent work?

After solving, rewrite your understanding in one sentence. If you cannot explain the change without reading the code line-by-line, you probably copied the shape without fully owning the idea.
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve(), "exercise 10.2: implementation is project-specific") }

examples.output: A working Go program or function demonstrating the requested behavior.

problems.title: Exercise 10.3: Using fetch http gopl io
problems.statement: Using fetch http://gopl.io/ch1/helloworld?go-get=1, find out which service hosts the code samples for this book. (HTTP requests from go get include the go-get parameter so that servers can distinguish them from ordinary browser requests.)

problems.title: Exercise 10.4: tool that reports the set
problems.statement: Construct a tool that reports the set of all packages in the workspace that tran- sitively depend on the packages specified by the arguments. Hint: you will need to run go list twice, once for the initial packages and once for all packages. You may want to parse its JSON output using the encoding/json package (§4.5).

problems.title: Exercise 11.1: Char Classes
problems.statement: Count letters, digits, and spaces in a string.
problems.lesson:
This entry follows Exercise 11.1 from The Go Programming Language.

Where it sits in the book: Chapter 11, Testing. This chapter is about test functions, table tests, random testing, coverage, benchmarks, profiling, and examples.

What the book is training here: not just syntax, but the habit of reading a small real program, changing one moving part, and observing the consequence. Many exercises in this book are intentionally not LeetCode puzzles. They are closer to real Go work: adjust a command-line tool, process files, stream HTTP responses, parse text, write a test, or make concurrent code behave.

How to study it:
1. First identify the original program or concept the exercise is extending.
2. Name the exact behavior that changes.
3. Keep the rest of the program boring and unchanged.
4. Run it with a tiny input before trying a large input.
5. If the task involves I/O, errors, or concurrency, make the failure case visible.

Mental model: Go code is usually easiest when each value has one job. Inputs come from args/files/network, the program transforms them with simple loops/functions, and errors are checked at the boundary where they happen.

Judgeable conversion: this book exercise has been reshaped into a deterministic Solve-style task so you can practice the same concept with local tests. It still follows the book's idea, but avoids command-line/filesystem/network details that are awkward for an in-browser judge.
problems.approach:
Book-following attack plan for Exercise 11.1:

Original exercise idea, paraphrased: Build tests for the charcount program in Section 4.3.

Do this like the book expects:
- Locate the relevant example program/concept in the chapter.
- Rebuild the smallest working version first.
- Make one change at a time.
- Prefer standard library tools over clever custom code.
- Print or return intermediate values while learning, then clean up.

If this is open-ended or experimental, your “accepted” proof is not a hidden LeetCode test; it is a short reproducible run that demonstrates the behavior the exercise asks for.

In this judgeable version, focus on the pure transformation inside Solve. That is the core logic the original full program would rely on.

Ignoring errors because the happy path worked once.
Treating open-ended book exercises like single-output puzzles instead of reproducible programs.

problems.explanation:
Why this exercise exists:

The book uses this task to connect a Go concept to an actual program. The important part is not memorizing the final code. The important part is seeing how Go's standard library and simple control flow combine into useful tools.

What to watch while solving:
- Where does data enter the program?
- What type represents it?
- Is the program building a result in memory, streaming it, or printing as it goes?
- What errors can happen, and where should they be handled?

After solving, rewrite your understanding in one sentence. If you cannot explain the change without reading the code line-by-line, you probably copied the shape without fully owning the idea.
problems.solutionCode:
import "unicode"
func Solve(s string) map[string]int { m:=map[string]int{"letters":0,"digits":0,"spaces":0}; for _,r:=range s { if unicode.IsLetter(r){m["letters"]++}; if unicode.IsDigit(r){m["digits"]++}; if unicode.IsSpace(r){m["spaces"]++} }; return m }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve("Go 1"),map[string]int{"letters":2,"digits":1,"spaces":1}) }

examples.output: letters=2 digits=1 spaces=1

problems.title: Exercise 11.2: set of tests for IntSet
problems.statement: Build a set of tests for IntSet (§6.5) that checks that its behavior after each operation is equivalent to a set based on built-in maps. Save your implementation for benchmarking in Exercise 11.7.

problems.title: Exercise 11.7: benchmarks for UnionWith and other
problems.statement: Build benchmarks for Extend, UnionWith, and other methods of *IntSet (§6.5) using large pseudo-random inputs. How fast can you make these methods run? How does the choice of word size affect performance? How fast is IntSet compared to a set implementation based on the built-in map type?

problems.title: Exercise 12.1: Display Kind
problems.statement: Return reflect kind string for any value.
problems.lesson:
This entry follows Exercise 12.1 from The Go Programming Language.

Where it sits in the book: Chapter 12, Reflection. This chapter is about reflect.Type, reflect.Value, display functions, struct tags, S-expressions, and dynamic value inspection.

problems.solutionCode:
import "reflect"
func Solve(v any) string { if v==nil {return "invalid"}; return reflect.TypeOf(v).Kind().String() }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve(3),"int"); assertEqual(Solve("x"),"string"); assertEqual(Solve([]int{}),"slice"); assertEqual(Solve(nil),"invalid") }

examples.output: slice

problems.title: Exercise 12.3: the missing cases of the
problems.statement: Implement the missing cases of the encode function. Encode booleans as t and nil, floating-point numbers using Go’s notation, and complex numbers like 1+2i as #C(1.0 2.0). Interfaces can be encoded as a pair of a type name and a value, for instance ("[]int" (1 2 3)), but beware that this notation is ambiguous: the reflect.Type.String method may return the same string for different types.

problems.title: Exercise 13.1: Size Class
problems.statement: Classify byte size as tiny (<=1), small (<=8), medium (<=64), or large.
problems.lesson:
This entry follows Exercise 13.1 from The Go Programming Language.

Where it sits in the book: Chapter 13, Low-Level Programming. This chapter is about unsafe.Sizeof, Alignof, Offsetof, unsafe.Pointer, cgo, and when low-level tricks are justified.

What the book is training here: not just syntax, but the habit of reading a small real program, changing one moving part, and observing the consequence. Many exercises in this book are intentionally not LeetCode puzzles. They are closer to real Go work: adjust a command-line tool, process files, stream HTTP responses, parse text, write a test, or make concurrent code behave.

How to study it:
1. First identify the original program or concept the exercise is extending.
2. Name the exact behavior that changes.
3. Keep the rest of the program boring and unchanged.
4. Run it with a tiny input before trying a large input.
5. If the task involves I/O, errors, or concurrency, make the failure case visible.

Mental model: Go code is usually easiest when each value has one job. Inputs come from args/files/network, the program transforms them with simple loops/functions, and errors are checked at the boundary where they happen.

Judgeable conversion: this book exercise has been reshaped into a deterministic Solve-style task so you can practice the same concept with local tests. It still follows the book's idea, but avoids command-line/filesystem/network details that are awkward for an in-browser judge.
problems.approach:
Book-following attack plan for Exercise 13.1:

Original exercise idea, paraphrased: Define a deep comparison function that considers numbers (of any type) equal if they differ by less than one part in a billion.

Do this like the book expects:
- Locate the relevant example program/concept in the chapter.
- Rebuild the smallest working version first.
- Make one change at a time.
- Prefer standard library tools over clever custom code.
- Print or return intermediate values while learning, then clean up.

If this is open-ended or experimental, your “accepted” proof is not a hidden LeetCode test; it is a short reproducible run that demonstrates the behavior the exercise asks for.

In this judgeable version, focus on the pure transformation inside Solve. That is the core logic the original full program would rely on.

Ignoring errors because the happy path worked once.
Treating open-ended book exercises like single-output puzzles instead of reproducible programs.

problems.solutionCode: func Solve(size uintptr) string { if size<=1{return "tiny"}; if size<=8{return "small"}; if size<=64{return "medium"}; return "large" }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve(1),"tiny"); assertEqual(Solve(8),"small"); assertEqual(Solve(64),"medium"); assertEqual(Solve(65),"large") }

examples.output: small
